coredata.dc:title: Efficacious sensing and elimination of iron ions from aqueous medium by polyethylenimine functionalized carbon dot

Anitha, K.
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, Iron (Fe) ions are detected and removed from an aqueous medium using a green emissive polyethylenimine functionalized carbon dots (GPC), which are produced hydrothermally and have high adsorption efficiency and sensitivity. As prepared, GPC uses an adsorption approach to remove iron ions and a Turn-Off mechanism to detect iron ions. A UV-Vis and photoluminescence spectroscopy are used to evaluate the results of several experiments, including those involving the interference of other ions, changing the concentration of metal ions, pH, reaction time, and the dosage of adsorbent in the sensor, in an attempt to achieve high sensitivity (with a lower limit of detection of 0.08 ppm) and high adsorption efficiency of approximately 98 % (8 µM of Fe and 1 ppm of GPC) towards iron ions. The isotherm and kinetic analyses of adsorption show that pseudo-second-order kinetics and Freundlich isotherm suits the experimental data better. With a removal percentage of roughly 89 percent after three cycles, desorption analysis highlights its regeneration efficiency. The mechanism of adsorption and quenching are extensively investigated; The static and dynamic quenching mechanisms are supported by high static constant value of approximately 23.5 ×103 M and the lifetime decay declination of 2.42 ns, respectively. Zeta potential measurements and XPS analysis are used to examine the electrostatic interaction between GPC and Fe in the adsorption mechanism. Ultimately, the prepared material's contentment results illustrate its potential for practical applicability with five actual water samples.

head.ce:title: Efficacious sensing and elimination of iron ions from aqueous medium by polyethylenimine functionalized carbon dot

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, Iron (Fe) ions are detected and removed from an aqueous medium using a green emissive polyethylenimine functionalized carbon dots (GPC), which are produced hydrothermally and have high adsorption efficiency and sensitivity. As prepared, GPC uses an adsorption approach to remove iron ions and a Turn-Off mechanism to detect iron ions. A UV-Vis and photoluminescence spectroscopy are used to evaluate the results of several experiments, including those involving the interference of other ions, changing the concentration of metal ions, pH, reaction time, and the dosage of adsorbent in the sensor, in an attempt to achieve high sensitivity (with a lower limit of detection of 0.08 ppm) and high adsorption efficiency of approximately 98 % (8 µM of Fe and 1 ppm of GPC) towards iron ions. The isotherm and kinetic analyses of adsorption show that pseudo-second-order kinetics and Freundlich isotherm suits the experimental data better. With a removal percentage of roughly 89 percent after three cycles, desorption analysis highlights its regeneration efficiency. The mechanism of adsorption and quenching are extensively investigated; The static and dynamic quenching mechanisms are supported by high static constant value of approximately 23.5 ×103 M and the lifetime decay declination of 2.42 ns, respectively. Zeta potential measurements and XPS analysis are used to examine the electrostatic interaction between GPC and Fe in the adsorption mechanism. Ultimately, the prepared material's contentment results illustrate its potential for practical applicability with five actual water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is one of the most vital requirements for all beings. It is crucial to preserve a healthy environment for all living things. Global freshwater crises are rapidly getting worse due to the enormous growth of human populations, civilizations, and industrialization [1]. Approximately 700 million people are unable to use clean water for their daily needs as a result of this freshwater shortage. Numerous dangerous pollutants, including organic materials, radioactive nucleating metals, and heavy metals, are the cause of the water poisoning [2]. Heavy metal ions are the most harmful and non-degradable of all contaminants. It tends to biologically accumulate depending on the outcome of the food chain. It also poses a concern to human safety and adversely affects aquatic ecosystems [3]. Therefore, everyone has a primary responsibility to safeguard fresh water before it becomes contaminated, and if contamination happens, remediation is required.

Iron (Fe), is the element that is most widely available on Earth and the human body relies significantly on it [4]. Insufficient or excessive amounts of iron in the body can lead to various health problems. For instance, iron shortage causes anaemia and liver cirrhosis, whereas excessive iron causes anorexia and other age-related diseases such as heart disease, liver and kidney damage, etc. [5,6]. Iron plays a significant role in industrialization and is a suitable industrial product. Iron can easily oxidize in the air, and its deposition on pipelines poses a threat to the water environment [5]. Excessive iron content in water can alter its colour, taste, and odour, and when it exceeds the permissible levels set by environmental agencies like the World Health Organization (WHO), the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), and the European Medical Agency (EMA), it can seriously harm both the environment and human health [7]. Therefore, it is imperative to create a material with high adsorption efficiency and high sensitivity that has a high removal percentage and a lower detection limit than what is permitted [8].

All sustaining problems of water pollution by iron ions motivate us to detect and remove it from the aqueous medium. It is, therefore, more desirable to apply the fluorescence detection method rather than absorption, reflection, and other optical detection methods due to its advantages, which include fast response times [3,9], and unique features including the ability to identify the target-heavy metal ion with the naked eye [10]. To remove heavy metal ions from an aqueous medium adsorption method is elected rather than chemical precipitation, ion exchange, reverse osmosis, electro dialysis, ultrafiltration, coagulation, etc. due to its inexpensive, simple, residue-free, and efficiency [11]. Furthermore, other techniques for removing heavy metal ions are not frequently used because of their high expense, poor effectiveness, high chemical demand, power consumption, etc. [12].

The semiconductor carbon quantum dots (CDs) are used as a fluorophore in this work because of their unique characteristics, which include high photostability, low photobleaching, broad absorption spectra, and superior fluorescence properties. Perhaps most importantly, they are non-toxic compared to other materials that have been described [13,14]. For instance, on detecting ferric ions, wang et al. reported a pyrene fluorescent probe 1,3,6,8-pyrene tetra sulfonic acid tetrasodium salt with a limit of detection of 9 µg/l [5]. Chang et al., established carbon dots with a limit of detection of 0.025 µM [4]. Liu et al. proposed a dual emissive carbon dot with a limit of detection of 0.8 µmol/l for Fe [15]. Therefore to improve the features of the carbon quantum dots, they are functionalized by polyethylenimine (PEI) as a polymer that helps to enhance its properties [16] and to recognize target analyte with the help of amine groups in PEI [17] to improve its high sensitivity and high adsorption efficiency than the reported materials such as Jordanian natural zeolite proposed by al-amber et al., with adsorption efficiency of 70 % (1 g adsorbent per 50 ml and 400 ppm of Fe) [18]. Tahir et al. reported bentonite clay with an adsorption efficiency of 98 % (0.5 g of bentonite and 100 mg/l of Fe) [19]. In brief, one of the main causes to employ carbon dot are its biocompatibility and low toxicity, as previously noted [20]. Its characteristics are further improved by using poyethylenimine, as it is hydrophilic and encourages interaction with heavy metal ions by interacting with amino groups [21]. Lately, researchers have been effectively drawn to the detection and removal of heavy metal ions using single materials. For instance, Li, Ming, et al. created cellulose-based hybrid hydrogel beads with carbon dots for the detection and removal of mercury ions [22]. Therefore, the current work has a lot of potential because it simultaneously detects and removes iron ions rather than doing it separately as prior studies have shown.

Herein, Polyethylenimine functionalized carbon dots are synthesized by hydrothermal method, characterized, and used to simultaneously detect and eliminate iron ions from the aqueous medium. In addition, the generated GPC nanomaterial's fluorescence sensing and adsorption efficiency are determined by calculating its Limit of Detection (LOD), percentage of removal efficiency, adsorption isotherms, and adsorption kinetics, respectively. By analyzing it with actual water samples, its practicality potential is ascertained.

Citric acid (CA), ascorbic acid (AA), polyethylenimine (PEI) solution, phosphate standard for IC–buffer solution, cadmium acetate dihydrate, mercury (II) chloride, lead nitrate, barium chloride dihydrate, manganese (II) chloride tetrahydrate, copper (II) chloride dihydrate, chromium (III) chloride hexahydrate, aluminum chloride anhydrous, cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate, iron (II) sulphate heptahydrate, nickel (II) acetate, tin (II) chloride, zinc sulphate heptahydrate, bismuth (III) chloride, silver nitrate, strontium chloride hexahydrate, magnesium chloride hexahydrate, tellurium (IV) oxide, and distilled water were purchased as the analytical grade from Sigma-Aldrich chemicals private limited, Bangalore, India and utilized without any further purification.

The powder XRD patterns were obtained using a PANalytical X-pert pro diffractometer using CuKα radiation(λ=1.540 Å) operated at a voltage of 40 kV and current of 30 mA. SHIMADZU UV-2450 spectrophotometer was used to record absorption spectra, photoluminescence spectra were obtained from SHIMADZU RF-5301 spectrofluorometer, Fourier transform infrared data was recorded by using SHIMADZU FTIR-8400S, Raman spectrum was obtained using confocal Raman microscope (WiTec alpha 300, Germany). The morphology of the samples was analyzed using a scanning electron microscope (TESCAN VEGA3 SBH, 30 kV) and a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (Tecnai G2 20 S-TWIN TEM, 200 kV). The elemental composition of the samples was confirmed by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy measurements were carried out by utilizing an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (K-Alpha-KAN9954133) with energy range from 100 to 4000 eV. JOBIN YUON Fluorolog-3–11 (Pulsed-Diode source – 340 nm) was utilized to record the life-time measurement of the fluorophore. Zeta potential measurement was done by Malvern Zetasizer MAL1052893.

GPC was synthesized via hydrothermal method [23], where citric acid, ascorbic acid, and polyethylenimine solution were the precursors. Initially, 0.15 g of ascorbic acid, 0.8 g of citric acid, and 0.18 g of polyethylenimine solution were allowed to dissolve in 10 ml of warm distilled water by using a magnetic stirrer for 15 mins. Once a homogeneous solution has been obtained, it is placed in an autoclave and kept at 150˚C for one hour. After allowing it to reach room temperature, ethanol is added to purify the mixture until it turns turbid. After that, the mixture is centrifuged for 20 min at 4000 rpm. Material that has been prepared can be kept for later use.

Sample solutions were prepared as follows to predict the selectivity of GPC towards fluorescence sensing response of different metal ions such as aluminium, barium, bismuth, cadmium, chromium, copper, cobalt, iron, lead, mercury, manganese, magnesium, nickel, strontium, silver, tin, tellurium, and zinc. The metal ions listed above were synthesized as stock solutions, with a known concentration of 1 µM. Then 50 µl stock solution of various metals was mixed individually with 500 µl of prepared GPC after diluting with 0.5 ml of buffer solution with a concentration of 10 mM [24]. Then the samples were analyzed to select the metal ion. To examine selectivity in the presence of other interfering metal ions, multiple metal ions were introduced to the GPC solution using the same procedure [25].

Initially, iron (II) sulphate heptahydrate was used to prepare stock solutions of iron ions at various concentrations from 5 µM to 600 µM. After diluting 400 µl of GPC with 0.5 ml of buffer solution (10 mM), 50 µl of various amounts of iron ion stock solution were added [24]. Subsequently, the fluorescence spectra of every sample were recorded.

In order to conduct the batch experiment, 0.1 mol/L of NaOH and HNO3 were added to the solution (GPC with metal ion) to change the pH, which was then measured using a pH meter [26]. Then by varying the concentration of GPC, the effect of adsorbent dosage on adsorption efficiency will be noticed. By using the sample preparation method for sensitivity, different concentrations of metal ions were introduced to GPC to examine the impacts of metal ion concentration on adsorption efficiency. To regenerate or reuse GPC, Fe ions are desorbed using sodium hydroxide solution as a desorption agent. Here, the pH of the GPC-Fe solution was raised from 6 by adding 0.5 mM of NaoH solution, following adsorption [27].

Water samples collected from the neighbourhood's taps, rivers, ponds, lakes, and wells. The real water samples were filtered via a 0.22 µm filter to remove the suspended particles. Subsequently, 0.5 µM and 1 µM of iron ions were added to the samples. Then, following the previously described technique (sample preparation for sensitivity), actual water samples were added to the GPC solution [24].

The disorderness of polyethylenimine carbon dots is indicated by a diffraction peak at 22.5˚ ( Fig. 1(a)), which corresponds to (002) plane and indicates that GPC is amorphous [28,29]. Their particle size is relatively small, evidenced by the broad diffraction peak of GPC [30]. Additionally, the Scherrer equation [31] is used to calculate the size of GPC,
(1) D = kλ/βcosθ
where λ is the X-ray wavelength in nanometer (nm), β is the full-width half maximum of the diffraction peak in radians, K is the Scherrer constant (0.94) and θ is the half of 2θ, the angle of diffraction. On substituting the values in the above relation, the particle size of GPC is calculated as 2.13 nm.

Scanning electron microscopy is used to analyze the morphology of GPC (SEM). As seen in Fig. 1(b), the produced GPC has quasi-spherical morphology predicated on the SEM image. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image clearly shows that as prepared GPC has spherical morphology (Fig. 1(c)) with average particle size of about 3.6–4 nm (Fig. 1(d)).

Using a UV-Vis absorption spectrophotometer and a photoluminescence spectrophotometer, the optical characteristics of the produced green emissive polyethylenimine carbon dots, such as absorption, emission, and lifetime, are examined. As prepared GPC shows a broad absorption peak ( Fig. 2(a)) centered at 355 nm may be caused by the n-π* transition of CO [32]. The band gap energy of GPC is calculated using the Tauc plot by making use of absorption data and the corresponding band gap energy of GPC is determined as 2.9 eV as shown in Fig. 2(a)-inset. Thus the reduced band gap of GPC is due to an increase in the size of the carbon dot. Based on its size and band gap its emission colour is green when irradiated under a UV lamp at 360 nm (inset in Fig. 2(b)). Each property such as size, band gap, and emission colour of the prepared carbon dot is correlated based on the quantum confinement effect [33]. Fig. 2(b) shows the photoluminescence spectra of GPC, which has a strong broad emission band between 390 nm to 610 nm centered at 490 nm when excited at 360 nm [34]. This high emission peak can be related to the low band gap energy. The stability of the prepared GPC is well noticed with the time optimization process of GPC with an analyte, in which GPC shows stable emission when irradiated for a long time. The average lifetime decay of GPC is determined as 6.12 ns. Thus the lifetime is decreased by 2.42 ns when metal ion is incorporated with GPC (Fig. 2(c)), which shows its quenching behaviour towards iron ions [35].

The functional groups of GPC are revealed by FTIR analysis. Here, FTIR spectra are recorded in the range of 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1 as shown in Fig. 2(d). Peaks at 3731 cm−1and 3443 cm−1, which are sharp and broad peak correspond to the stretching vibration of O–H [32]. The peaks at 2979 cm−1 and 2883 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibration of C–H [32,36]. A weak peak at 2825 cm−1 is assigned to the vibration of C–H from the aldehyde group [37]. The arise of the peak at 2319 cm−1 is due to the presence of atmosphere carbon-di-oxide [38]. Peaks at 1643 cm−1 confirms the presence of stretching and bending vibration of the N–H bond [39] and 1454 cm−1 peak resembles the vibration of C–H bond [30]. The stretching vibration of C–O–C appears at 1361 cm−1 [32] and a peak at 1037 cm−1 corresponds to the bending of C–O [39]. The peak appears from 703 cm−1 to 517 cm−1 assigned to the stretching vibration of CH2 and C–N [29]. The interaction between polyethylenimine and carbon dot, which improves the characteristics of carbon dot, is represented by the occurrence of the N-H bond [39]. A significant site for adsorption is the O–H bond [40]. The alcohol group may have appeared at the peak at 3731 cm−1 as a result of the complete reaction between OH from ascorbic acid with NH2, whereas the O–H group from CDs may have originated from OH of citric acid [41]. The water solubility of carbon dots is revealed by the N-H bond [36].

GPC is further investigated using Raman spectroscopy, which shows that it possesses distinctive D and G band peaks at 1370 cm−1 and 1579 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 2(e)). The relative intensity (ID/IG) ratio between the D and G bands is determined to be 0.98, indicating that the produced green emissive GPC has a poor crystalline structure and is disordered [29].

The surface elemental analysis of green-emitting polyethyleneimine functionalized carbon dots is studied using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). XPS spectrum ( Fig. 3(a)), shows three primary peaks at 286.05 eV, 400.34 eV, and 532.3 eV representing C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively, that indicates the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen on the surface of GPC [29,36]. The C1s band in the high-resolution spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks as shown in Fig. 3(b), which were identified as sp2 carbons (CC/C–C, 284.15 eV), sp3 carbons (C-O/C-N, 286.1 eV), and carboxyl carbons (O–CO, 288.1 eV). The N1s spectrum exhibits two peaks (Fig. 3(d), according to the N–C and N–H groups, at 399.8 eV and 399.5 eV, respectively. Two peaks may be seen at sp2 (C = O, 531.3 eV) and sp3 (C–O, 532.3 eV) in the O1s peaks (Fig. 3(c)). All of these XPS results have been confirmed by FTIR data.

EDX analysis is used to characterize the elemental composition of GPC. Fig. 2(f), indicates three elements, namely carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, together constitute 30.11 %, 21.38 %, and 48.52 % of the weight percentage of prepared GPC, respectively [36,42].

Bright green emission with intense fluorescence occurs in the synthesized polyethylenimine carbon dots. To ascertain the selectivity towards a specific metal ion based on the metal ions fluorescence emission response, different metal ions have been added to the material GPC in the selectivity technique. The fluorescence enhancement towards the produced GPC is observed in all metal ions except for Fe, which displays low fluorescence. Unfortunately, iron ions are the sole thing that quenches the green emission of polyethyleneimine carbon dots. Thus, iron ions are chosen as a target analyte based on the quenching response of Fe towards GPC. This confirms that the produced material GPC, as depicted in Fig. 4(a), has a high sensitivity toward iron ions. As a result, GPC implements an exclusive fluorescence "turn-off" mechanism to detect iron.

Analyzing the interference of other metal ions into the synthesized GPC in addition to the iron ions can help to actively support the selectivity of iron ions [25]. Here, iron and foreign metal ions are included in equal amounts to the prepared material, and the sample's fluorescence responses are then noted. As seen in Fig. 4(b), the fluorescence response indicates that the fluorescence of GPC is quenched when iron ions from other foreign metal ions interfere with it. This demonstrates that even in the presence of other metal ions, the turn-off mechanism is still capable of recognizing iron ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensor (GPC) optimization to sense Fe ions
ce:section.ce:para: Optimization of several experimental parameters, including pH, response time, Fe content, and GPC concentration, is required to improve GPC's sensing capacity [43]. The sensor's performance may be impacted by several factors. Therefore, by adjusting these parameters, the GPC sensitivity was studied. During GPC optimization, the pH of the GPC solution was adjusted from pH 5 to pH 8 Fig. 4(c). Since adopting low acid and high basic areas causes the adsorption of hydrogen ions and the precipitation of iron ions, respectively, pH 6 has been determined to be the ideal pH condition for this investigation [44,45]. Concerning the above, low acid and high basic pH are neglected. The fluorescence response is then measured at various intervals of time to determine the response time of the GPC with metal ions. Here, fluorescence is measured every 5 mins intervals for about 35 mins. However, as the detection period is increased, the fluorescence signal does not significantly change (Fig. 4(d)), indicating that the fluorescence sensor is photostable. This photostability demonstrates that the fluorescence of the iron-ion-containing material may be monitored in less than 30 min, which is sufficient to set up some preliminary measurements. Additionally, the sensor has been examined by altering GPC and iron ion concentrations. Because of their interaction, GPC exhibits a fluorescence quenching reaction to iron ions during the selection process. Hence, it exhibits a strong fluorescence quenching reaction when the concentration of GPC is maintained while the concentration of iron ions is raised, and the fluorescence increases as the GPC concentration rises as shown in Fig. 4(e). This may be due to the static or dynamic quenching of the fluorophore and iron ions [14]. Hence, the sensitivity of GPC was determined effectively by making use of these optimized experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of GPC towards Fe ion
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate GPC sensitivity to iron ions, various concentrations of iron ions from 5 µM to 600 µM were incorporated in GPC solution. As a result, as the iron ion concentration increases, the nanomaterial's fluorescence intensity steadily decreases ( Fig. 5(a) and (d)), correspondingly its absorbance increases as shown in Fig. 5(b). The correlation between the concentration of iron ions and the fluorescence quenching (I
0
/I) was studied with the obtained data. Where I
0 represents the fluorescence intensity before the incorporation of iron ion and I represent the fluorescence intensity after the incorporation of iron ion. A linear calibration curve is plotted from 80 µM to 330 µM (Fig. 5(c) - inset). It shows a correlation coefficient R2 equal to 0.988 and the lower detection limit was determined as 288 nM, using LOD = 3σ/s, where ‘σ’ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and s represents the slope of the calibration curve [24]. Thus, green emissive polyethylenimine carbon dots ascertained an extremely high sensitivity towards iron ions, as seen by their low detection limit of 0.08 ppm. The comparative analysis with the literature is given in Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of iron ion via adsorption
ce:section.ce:para:
Through a series of batch experiments, the removal percentage of iron ions is determined to evaluate the adsorption efficiency [12,48,49]. To determine the adsorption efficiency, three controlled experiments are conducted by varying the pH of a solution, dosage of adsorbent and concentration of Fe ions as follows. UV-Vis spectrophotometer (UV) is used to measure the concentration of iron ions. The increased number of available adsorbent sites for adsorbate (iron ions) will result in a higher removal percentage of iron ions. The following formulas were used to determine the adsorbent's adsorption capacity and efficiency [12,18],
(2)

Where V is the working solution volume in liters, W is the mass of the adsorbent in grams, and C

1 is the final concentration of metal ions. By following, the comparative study of previously reported adsorbents with GPC is shown in Table 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH on adsorption efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of the solution has a fundamental impact on the metal ion (Fe) adsorption effectiveness or removal percentage. One can mistakenly predict the metal ion's adsorption efficiency when the pH is incorrect. An adsorbent's adsorption active site can be affected by the pH of the solution. Here, the influence of pH was examined by altering the solution's pH from 5 to 8 (pH), with an adsorbent dosage of 4 mg/l and an iron ion concentration of 33 µM. In this case, the pH ranged from 5 to 8 to analyze the adsorption efficacy in a slightly acidic and slightly basic region for solution handling that is user-friendly. As a result, as shown in Fig. 6(a), it is observed that the removal efficiency of iron ions increased from 88.6 % to cent percent with an increase in pH from 5 to 6 accordingly. The removal efficiency subsequently reduced after pH 6. Lower pH, or the acidic zone, results in reduced adsorption efficiency because hydrogen ions are mostly adsorbed on the GPC nanocomposite at these levels [44]. There is no significant increase in adsorption efficiency above pH 6. Adsorption efficiency steadily decreases at high pH levels as a result of the abrupt precipitation of Fe ions on the basic area caused by a formation of metal hydroxide [44,45]. Thus, the ideal pH was determined to be 6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of initial concentration of Fe ions on adsorption efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: GPC's removal efficiency is observed upon comparing the concentration of iron ions. Maintaining a pH of 6 and utilizing solutions of known concentration (C0) of iron ions from 33 µM to 297 µM incorporated into the GPC nanomaterial with a dosage of about 4 mg/l. After adsorption, the final concentration (C1) of the iron ion in the working solution is noticed. Consequently, the adsorption capacity rises from 6.88 mg/g to 20.43 mg/g (concentration from 33 µM to 165 µM) and the removal efficiency falls from 98.79 % to 4.3 % (concentration from 33 µM to 297 µM). However, the adsorption capacity falls from 5 mg/g to 3.09 mg/g (concentration from 231 µM to 297 µM). The observed result indicates that the removal efficiency decreases as the concentration of iron ions increases because there are fewer available adsorbent adsorption sites. In this case, the adsorption capacity increases for the concentration from 33 µM to 165 µM and then decreases. This is because, at first, there are a lot of available adsorption vacant sites, which causes the adsorption capacity to increase for a specific amount of concentration of iron ions. After a certain concentration, the adsorption capacity tends to decrease due to a lack of adsorption sites on adsorbents, which can be related to a decrease in the iron ion removal percentage [51] (Fig. 6(b)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of adsorbent dosage on adsorption efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: Different ratios of GPC nanomaterial to Fe concentration, or GPC:Fe (mg/l), such as 4:1, 4:3, 4:4, and 4:5 are analyzed while keeping pH 6 to investigate the adsorption efficiency on the effect of adsorbent dosage. As the adsorbent dosage increases from 4:5–4:1, the removal efficiency rises from 59 % to 98.79 % and the adsorption capacity falls from 20.4 mg/g to 6.88 mg/g, as illustrated in Fig. 6(c). It is assumed that when the dosage of iron ions falls, the surface area of an adsorbent increases, leading to a high metal ion removal efficiency [26]. By either raising the dosage of the adsorbent or lowering the concentration of iron ions, the prepared GPC nanomaterial exhibits remarkable adsorption efficiency.

To determine the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent, effective equilibrium adsorption isotherm models, such as the Freundlich and Langmuir models, were utilized [52]. In short, the amount of adsorbate that is adsorbed on the adsorbent can be measured by the adsorption capacity [26]. As a result, the Langmuir and Freundlich models were employed to determine the characteristics of adsorption. The Langmuir (4) and Freundlich (5) models are represented, respectively, by the following equations [53].
(4)
q
e
= Q
m
K
L
C

/ 1+K
L
C

(5)
q
e
= K
F
C

1/n

The linear forms of the Langmuir (6) and Freundlich (7) models [54] can be used to calculate the adsorption parameters. They are as follows,
(6) 1/qe = (1/KLQm. 1/Ce + 1/Qm)
(7) Log qe = log KF + 1/n log Ce
Where q
e is the adsorption capacity (mg/g), C
e is the equilibrium concentration of adsorbate (iron ions) in the solution (mg/L), Q
m is the maximum amount of adsorbate adsorbed on the adsorbent, K
L represents Langmuir constant which is the enthalpy of the adsorption, K
F represents Freundlich constant and 1/n is the intensity of adsorption.

The results of the Langmuir and Freundlich models' analyses of the adsorption parameters based on experimental data (batch experiments) are provided in Table 3. With a greater linear regression coefficient for the Freundlich adsorption isotherm than the Langmuir isotherm (Fig. 6(d)), the iron ion adsorption on GPC in this case is multilayer adsorption [54]. Iron ion removal from the contaminated water causes physical adsorption [55].

Adsorption kinetics is the study of how the system's sorption characteristics vary over time [56]. As a result, the primary factor influencing the adsorption process is the contact period between the adsorbent and adsorbate. Here, the pseudo first-order and pseudo second-order kinetics models were used to assess the iron adsorption rate on GPC. The relationship between pseudo-first-order kinetics and pseudo-second-order kinetics is represented by Eqs. (8) and (9), respectively [52].
(8) ln (Qe-Qt) = lnQe-K1t
(9) t/Qt = (1/Qe)t+1/K2Qe 2
where Q
e and Q
t represent, respectively, the concentrations of adsorbed iron ions (mg/g) on the adsorbents' surface at equilibrium and time t. The first-order rate constant per minute is denoted by K
1 , while the second-order rate constant per minute is represented by K
2
. Consequently, the experimental data ( Table 4) fits far better with pseudo second-order kinetics than pseudo first-order kinetics as seen in Fig. 6(e), indicating that chemisorption rather than physisorption governs adsorption overall [57]. However, electrostatic interaction can be considered as a type of chemisorption as well [58]. Under these circumstances, adsorption is determined by adsorption capacity rather than adsorbate concentration [59]. But when it comes for elimination of iron ions, both chemical and physical adsorption occur. This is because polyethylenimine contains amine groups, which support both sorption processes because of their significant metal ion chelation capabilities [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Desorption of Fe ions from adsorbent
ce:section.ce:para: On performing the analysis, the pH of solution will reach the basic region. In basic region the metal will turn into metal hydroxide and gets precipitated. So that the solution of GPC with Fe ions, after reacting with desorption agent makes iron ions precipitate. Finally, the precipitated solution is further centrifuged and filtered with 0.22 µm filter. Then repeatedly (3 cycles) GPC is used to check its regeneration ability to remove iron ions effectively by determining its removal percentage. After three cycles, its removal efficiency is determined as 89 %. Thus the adsorbent has good regeneration efficiency [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practicability of GPC on real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental results demonstrate how effectively the GPC nanomaterial identifies and remove iron ions from real samples are shown in Table 5. Water from taps, rivers, lakes, ponds, and wells was gathered, filtered, and spiked with iron ions at known concentrations. The water was then allowed to record its fluorescence spectra and final concentration to determine its adsorption efficiency and fluorescence quenching (Turn-Off). Consequently, as the concentration of iron ions increases, the fluorescence quenching percentage increases and the adsorption efficiency decreases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism on detection and removal of iron ions
ce:section.ce:para:
The present study employs fluorescence sensing for iron ion detection and adsorption for iron ion removal. When GPC interacts with target iron ions during detection, a fluorescent "Turn-Off" mechanism is activated and Fe is removed when the adsorbents (iron ions) are adsorbed on the adsorbate (GPC). In this case, turn-off sensing is simply the fluorescence quenching property of fluorophore. The addition of Fe ions causes a decrease in the vibrant green emission of GPC, which is indicative of fluorescence quenching and may be the outcome of a complex formation between the quencher and fluorophore. This can be observed from the selectivity and sensitivity process. Their interaction may be the reason for this complex formation. However, there are three possible methods for quenching: static, dynamic, and inner filter effect [14,60]. Therefore, the Stern-Volmer (S-V) relation is employed to plot the I0/I vs. Fe concentration graph to analyze the quenching mechanism [61].,
(10) I0/I = 1+(KD+KS)[Q] + (KDKS) [Q]2
where K
D is the dynamic quenching constant, K
S is the static quenching constant, Q is the concentration of the quencher (Fe ions), and I
0 and I are the fluorescence intensity without and with quencher, respectively. A study of the S-V plot in Figure reveals that it has a second-order polynomial fit and deviates from linearity (Fig. 5(c)). The non-linearity of the S-V plot provides proof that the current work's quenching mechanism is both static and dynamic. When a fluorophore and quencher interact in the ground state or excited state, respectively, quenching happens in a static or dynamic method [62]. It is feasible to determine which quenching mechanism is more prevalent than others based on absorbance spectra, fluorescence lifetime decay, S-V plot response with Fe concentration, and quenching constant. When the absorbance spectra of the fluorophore and the fluorophore with the quencher are compared, it is seen that the absorbance intensity increases corresponding to the quencher's concentration whereas no new peaks have formed ( Fig. 7(a)). This behaviour demonstrates that no complexes develop when iron ions are added, supporting the evidence that the mechanism is related to dynamic quenching. [5]. Furthermore, the fluorophore's lifetime is compared with its lifetime in the presence of a quencher. It can identify a reduction in lifetime from 6.12 ns to 2.42 ns (Fig. 2(c)), which leads to a dynamic quenching mechanism because both lifetimes would remain constant in a static quenching mechanism [47,63]. Dynamic quenching mechanism contributes to the quenching process, as demonstrated by analyses of absorbance and lifetime studies.

Similar to the dynamic quenching mechanism, the static quenching mechanism satisfies specific circumstances and hence plays an important role in the current study. The S-V plot's upward curvature suggests the existence of a static quenching mechanism (Fig. 5(c)), in which iron ions and the fluorophore GPC interact to form a ground-state non-fluorescent complex [64]. Then the quenching constant such as Kd and KS is calculated using the above-mentioned Eq. (11), before based on the lifetime decay study, the dynamic quenching constant is calculated using the following relation [61],
(11) τ/τ0 = 1 + KD[Q]

In this case, τ represents the fluorescence lifetime while iron ions are present, and τ
0 represents the fluorescence lifetime when the ions are absent. By using the relation, the dynamic quenching constant is determined as 2.5 ×103 M, and the static dynamic quenching constant is calculated as 23.5 ×103 M. The higher static quenching constant value suggests that the static quenching mechanism works superior in the proposed work [65].

High UV light absorption of iron ions by itself and in interaction with GPC, respectively, as shown in Fig. 7(b) and (c), which confirm that the inner filter effect has a role on quenching green emission. When iron ions are added, the fluorescence of GPC reduces because Fe ions absorb UV radiation significantly more effectively than GPC does. This influences the quenching of fluorescence emission [60].

The adsorption mechanism, which involves an electrostatic interaction between anionic iron ions and cationic carbon dots, is also supported by this static quenching mechanism that leads to the formation of complexes at the ground state [66]. The surface charges of the fluorophore (GPC) and quencher (Fe) are determined by zeta potential analysis (Fig. 7(d,e)). Zeta potential indicates the cationic nature of GPC with the value of about 10.9 mV and Fe ions have anionic nature with the value of −0.5 mV. Thus, GPC adsorbs iron ions efficiently as a result of the electrostatic interaction.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy further supports the GPC-Fe ion interaction. In XPS study, the presence of amine groups at the binding energy of 399.5 eV in N1s spectrum serves as an active site to adsorb the heavy metal ions [67,68]. This occurs in such a way that positive amine groups in GPC promotes the electsostatic interaction with anionic iron ions [69]. It is concluded from an analysis of the fluorescence sensing and adsorption mechanism that this investigation involves both static and dynamic quenching. Static quenching promotes electrostatic contact, which is important for the efficient removal of iron ions, while dynamic quenching aids in the efficient sense of iron ions.

In conclusion, the GPC nano-material has been effectively synthesized to sense and eliminate iron ions from an aqueous medium. Polyethylenimine carbon dots have spherical morphology with an average particle size of about 3.6–4 nm. Its amorphous nature is also confirmed by the value of the relative intensity ratio calculated using the Raman analysis. The elemental analysis and functional groups of GPC are validated by EDX, FTIR, and XPS measurements, which show that GPC is an organic material, composed of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. This shows its toxic-less nature when compared with other inorganic quantum dots. GPC has a green emission at about 490 nm and its lifetime is determined as 6.12 ns.

GPC detects iron ions based on its fluorescence quenching mechanism, while GPC interacts with Fe ions. It has a low LOD of about 0.08 ppm, which is less than the permissible limit of the WHO (0.3 ppm), US-EPA (0.3 ppm), and EMA (0.2 ppm) [70]. Furthermore, GPC’s adsorption efficiency is determined using batch experiments, which indicate that GPC has high adsorption efficiency. In the isotherm study, Freundlich isotherm fits better than Langmuir isotherm, which shows that the adsorption is multilayer adsorption and correspondingly experimental data suits for pseudo-second-order kinetics.

Hence, it’s sensing and elimination mechanism is intensely discussed with the help of SV plot, absorbance, and lifetime analysis in the presence and absence of iron ions with a fluorophore, zeta potential, dynamic constant, static constant, and by using Rehm Weller relation. Thus the investigation suggests that quenching occurs by static and dynamic method, which also supports the adsorption mechanism. Finally, it is suggested that prepared GPC nano-material effectively detects and eliminates iron ions from the contaminated aqueous medium, this statement is sustained with the contribution of GPC on real sample analysis.
